topic: Someone discovers their mother kept every school assignment
raw_output:
[story 1]

The overflowing backpack sat on the kitchen counter like a monument to academic chaos. Liam stared at the mountain of crumpled papers, each one a testament to a year of parental oversight. He picked up a worksheet for ancient history, the ink bleeding slightly where he’d gripped it too tightly. "Seriously, Mom?" he muttered to the empty room, rubbing the bridge of his nose until it ached. He remembered the frantic search he’d undertaken for his calculus notes last Tuesday, only to find them tucked inside a library book from two years prior. It was like an elaborate, passive-aggressive system designed purely to induce stress. He sighed, a long, drawn-out sound that seemed to vibrate the very air. He just wanted a clean slate, a simple folder, not this archaeological dig of his scholastic life.

[story 2]

Sarah surveyed the overflowing filing cabinet in the spare room. It wasn't just papers; it was a veritable archive of her teenage years, neatly categorized by subject, date, and even the intensity of the required reading. A wave of sheer disbelief washed over her as she realized the sheer volume of work her mother had meticulously collected. "This is absurd," Sarah whispered, her voice tight with disbelief. She pulled out a stack of essays from sophomore year, the handwriting surprisingly neat for a collection this extensive. She ran a hand through her hair, feeling a familiar tightness in her chest. Why? Why would she hoard every single piece of paper? It felt like a deliberate, elaborate sabotage of her ability to simply *finish* things. She slammed the cabinet door shut, the sound echoing the frustration bubbling up inside her.

[story 3]

Mark leaned against the doorframe of his bedroom, his gaze fixed on the towering stacks of graded papers spilling out of his mother's study. He watched as she effortlessly pulled out a history project from 2018, a complete, annotated document. He felt a distinct tightening in his jaw. He’d spent an hour yesterday hunting for a specific chemistry lab report, only to discover it had been filed alongside permission slips and old holiday cards. He pushed off the frame, taking a slow, deliberate step toward the desk. "I don't understand the point," he said, his tone flat, betraying the churning annoyance beneath the surface. The sheer inefficiency of the situation was grating on his nerves; it was exhausting just to process the sheer volume of misplaced effort.
